elements.sent-2411-002: Temporal perception, the ability to detect and track objects over time, is critical in autonomous driving for maintaining a comprehensive understanding of dynamic environments.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, this task is hindered by significant challenges, including incomplete perception caused by occluded objects and observational blind spots, which are common in single-vehicle perception systems.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address these issues, we introduce LET-VIC, a LiDAR-based End-to-End Tracking framework for Vehicle-Infrastructure Cooperation (VIC).
elements.sent-2411-005: LET-VIC leverages Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication to enhance temporal perception by fusing spatial and temporal data from both vehicle and infrastructure sensors.
elements.sent-2411-006: First, it spatially integrates Bird's Eye View (BEV) features from vehicle-side and infrastructure-side LiDAR data, creating a comprehensive view that mitigates occlusions and compensates for blind spots.
elements.sent-2411-007: Second, LET-VIC incorporates temporal context across frames, allowing the model to leverage historical data for enhanced tracking stability and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-008: To further improve robustness, LET-VIC includes a Calibration Error Compensation (CEC) module to address sensor misalignments and ensure precise feature alignment.
elements.sent-2411-009: Experiments on the V2X-Seq-SPD dataset demonstrate that LET-VIC significantly outperforms baseline models, achieving at least a 13.7\% improvement in mAP and a 13.1\% improvement in AMOTA without considering communication delays.
elements.sent-2411-010: This work offers a practical solution and a new research direction for advancing temporal perception in autonomous driving through vehicle-infrastructure cooperation.
elements.sent-2411-011: Temporal perception, which encompasses both detection and tracking, is crucial for advanced autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-012: It enables Connected and Autonomous Vehicles (CAVs) to continuously identify and characterize objects while monitoring their dynamic changes over time, providing a detailed understanding of the dynamic environment.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, single-vehicle temporal perception systems face significant challenges, particularly in complex urban settings where occlusions and blind spots can limit the field of view.
elements.sent-2411-014: These limitations hinder the ability of CAVs to obtain a complete and reliable understanding of their surroundings.
elements.sent-2411-015: Vehicle-Infrastructure Cooperation (VIC), enabled by advancements in Cooperative Intelligent Transportation Systems (C-ITS) and Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication~\cite{chen2017vehicle,gyawali2020challenges,maaloul2021classification , offers a solution to the limitations of vehicle-only perception.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Through V2X, data can be exchanged seamlessly between CAVs and infrastructure, allowing the integration of infrastructure-based perception information.
elements.sent-2411-017: This cooperation enhances situational awareness and fills the gaps caused by occlusions or restricted views on the vehicle side.
elements.sent-2411-018: In particular, VIC strengthens temporal perception by providing a more holistic, continuous view of the surroundings, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:vic-diagram and Fig.~\ref{fig:vic-visual .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: By combining data from both vehicle and infrastructure sensors, VIC offers more accurate and robust temporal perception compared to standalone vehicle sensors.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{images/vic-diagram.png} \caption{Vehicle-Infrastructure Cooperative Diagram. The red car represents the Ego Vehicle. The vehicle-side LiDAR covers a semicircular area in front of the Ego Vehicle, while the infrastructure-side LiDAR has a fan-shaped coverage area. Through vehicle-infrastructure cooperation, the infrastructure can provide additional perception information to the vehicle.} \label{fig:vic-diagram}
elements.sent-2411-020: Despite the advantages of VIC, current VIC methods are limited in their use of temporal information.
elements.sent-2411-021: Existing approaches typically leverage temporal context only during the tracking stage, treating detection as an isolated task for each individual frame.
elements.sent-2411-022: This frame-by-frame detection fails to fully exploit the sequential information across frames, which could otherwise improve detection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-023: Moreover, most VIC approaches focus on spatial data fusion, integrating multi-view data from vehicle-side and infrastructure-side sensors at a single moment, without extending this fusion to encompass temporal information.
elements.sent-2411-024: Consequently, these approaches are limited by their reliance on instantaneous data, missing out on the benefits of historical context for more informed decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-025: An end-to-end approach to temporal perception, which utilizes sequential data across frames for both detection and tracking, offers a promising alternative to current VIC methods~\cite{zhang2023motiontrack,meinhardt2022trackformer,zeng2022motr,zhou2020tracking,yin2021center .}
elements.sent-2411-026: This paradigm enables a coherent understanding of object dynamics, resulting in a more robust perception system.
elements.sent-2411-027: When implemented with LiDAR technology, end-to-end temporal perception becomes even more effective.
elements.sent-2411-028: Compared to traditional camera-based systems, LiDAR provides precise distance measurements and maintains reliable performance in adverse weather and low-light conditions, making it an ideal foundation for advanced temporal perception.
elements.sent-2411-029: While LiDAR-based end-to-end temporal perception has shown strong potential in vehicle-only systems, extending this approach to a VIC-based framework that fully leverages both spatial and temporal contexts remains underdeveloped.
elements.sent-2411-030: Developing such a framework could significantly enhance safety and reliability in autonomous driving applications.

(a) illustrates perception using only infrastructure-side LiDAR, (b) illustrates perception using only vehicle-side LiDAR, and (c) shows the cooperative perception results combining (a) and (b). The red car represents the Ego Vehicle, green boxes indicate detected objects with their orientations, targets within the red dashed circles are those occluded from the vehicle's view, and targets within the black dashed circles are outside the vehicle's perception range.} \label{fig:vic-visual}
elements.sent-2411-031: In this study, we propose LET-VIC, the first LiDAR-based End-to-end Tracking framework for VIC, which designed to address major perception challenges in autonomous driving, including limited perception range and occlusion.
elements.sent-2411-032: LET-VIC fully utilizes LiDAR point clouds from both vehicle-side and infrastructure-side sensors, incorporating temporal data to greatly enhance object detection and tracking in complex traffic environments.
elements.sent-2411-033: The framework features an advanced feature fusion strategy that uses a VIC cross-attention mechanism to seamlessly integrate Bird's Eye View (BEV) features from the infrastructure-side into the vehicle-side BEV representation.
elements.sent-2411-034: Furthermore, We employ a Calibration Error Compensation (CEC) module to compensate for calibration errors between vehicle-side and infrastructure-side sensors, delivering precise and robust 3D object perception.
elements.sent-2411-035: Rigorous testing on the V2X-Seq-SPD dataset~\cite{yu2023v2x , which captures challenging real-world driving scenarios, demonstrates that LET-VIC consistently outperforms existing cooperative tracking methods.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-037: We introduce LET-VIC, a LiDAR-based End-to-end Tracking framework for VIC that leverages a novel VIC cross-attention mechanism to fuse BEV features from both vehicle and infrastructure side sensors, providing a unified solution for 3D object perception in complex traffic scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-038: We propose a self-supervised method to optimize spatial error correction during coordinate transformation.
elements.sent-2411-039: By learning offsets $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ within BEV queries, this method corrects misalignments caused by calibration errors, enhancing robustness against coordinate inaccuracies.
elements.sent-2411-040: LET-VIC consistently outperforms existing cooperative temporal perception methods on the V2X-Seq-SPD dataset~\cite{yu2023v2x .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Our experimental design also included evaluations under various delay conditions ranging from 0 ms to 300 ms, demonstrating LET-VIC's strong stability and adaptability, even though it was not explicitly optimized for delay handling.
elements.sent-2411-042: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-043: Section II reviews the related works relevant to this study.
elements.sent-2411-044: In Section III, we present LET-VIC, our proposed LiDAR-based end-to-end framework for VIC tracking.
elements.sent-2411-045: Section IV details the experimental results and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-046: Finally, Section V concludes the paper.
elements.sent-2411-047: With advancements in Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication technologies~\cite{sehla2022resource,cheng2022integrated,jang2023study,lu2024integrated , cooperative perception in autonomous driving enhances perception capabilities through data sharing and fusion across multiple agents.}
elements.sent-2411-048: This approach addresses challenges such as occlusion, long-distance perception, and sensor failures that single-vehicle perception may struggle with.
elements.sent-2411-049: Cooperative perception research primarily focuses on two types: Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) and Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) cooperation.
elements.sent-2411-050: In V2V cooperation, several methodologies have been explored.
elements.sent-2411-051: Early Fusion techniques, as seen in Cooper~\cite{chen2019cooper and DiscoNet~\cite{li2021learning , integrate raw sensor data from multiple vehicles, improving detection accuracy and extending perception range.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Feature Fusion approaches, such as OPV2V~\cite{xu2022opv2v , F-Cooper~\cite{chen2019f , V2VNet~\cite{wang2020v2vnet , and COOPERNAUT~\cite{cui2022coopernaut , combine intermediate-level features from multiple vehicles for enhanced perception.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Notably, How2comm~\cite{yang2023how2comm focuses on transmitting BEV features extracted from point clouds to enhance cooperative 3D object detection.}
elements.sent-2411-054: In contrast, V2I cooperation has been explored through models such as V2X-ViT~\cite{xu2022v2x , FFNet~\cite{yu2023flow , CTCE~\cite{zhong2024leveraging and V2X-Graph~\cite{ruan2023learning , which adopt a middle-fusion approach to integrate data from both vehicles and infrastructure.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: These models account for challenges like latency and calibration errors, primarily focusing on object detection tasks.
elements.sent-2411-056: While V2V cooperation benefits from richer shared data among vehicles, V2I is especially effective in scenarios where infrastructure sensors complement vehicle perception, providing broader environmental awareness.
elements.sent-2411-057: These diverse approaches underscore significant efforts to leverage collective sensing capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-058: These capabilities help overcome the inherent limitations of individual vehicle sensors, ultimately enhancing the reliability and safety of autonomous driving systems.
elements.sent-2411-059: While many studies focus on specific perception tasks, integrating cooperative strategies into broader end-to-end systems remains a promising direction for future research, offering the potential for more comprehensive system performance in complex driving environments.
elements.sent-2411-060: The paradigm of End-to-End Temporal Perception has redefined sequential object perception by shifting from traditional tracking-by-detection to a more integrated approach.
elements.sent-2411-061: This approach combines object detection and tracking within a unified model.
elements.sent-2411-062: Models such as MotionTrack~\cite{zhang2023motiontrack , TrackFormer~\cite{meinhardt2022trackformer , MOTR~\cite{zeng2022motr , CenterTrack~\cite{zhou2020tracking , and CenterPoint~\cite{yin2021center exemplify this shift, with each model contributing to multi-object temporal perception through different methods, such as sensor fusion, transformer-based architectures, and point tracking.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: MotionTrack~\cite{zhang2023motiontrack introduces a transformer-based end-to-end temporal perception algorithm utilizing LiDAR-camera fusion, setting a new benchmark for tracking multiple object classes with multimodal sensor inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-064: TrackFormer~\cite{meinhardt2022trackformer and MOTR~\cite{zeng2022motr reformulate temporal perception as a set prediction problem, leveraging transformer architectures to maintain track identity and spatiotemporal trajectories without requiring separate motion or appearance modeling.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: CenterTrack~\cite{zhou2020tracking simplifies the process by performing detection and tracking simultaneously, significantly improving temporal perception efficiency and accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-066: CenterPoint~\cite{yin2021center , a LiDAR-based 3D object detection and tracking framework, introduces an anchor-free approach that directly predicts object center points and regresses their attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-067: While end-to-end temporal perception offers substantial improvements in efficiency and accuracy, these existing methods generally lack LiDAR-based multi-view information fusion, which can limit their perception capabilities in complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-068: To address this gap, we propose a novel LiDAR-based end-to-end temporal perception framework for VIC that leverages multi-view fusion between vehicle-side and infrastructure-side sensors.
elements.sent-2411-069: This framework enhances perception by integrating complementary spatial and temporal information, enabling more accurate and robust temporal perception, demonstrating a marked advancement in the domain of cooperative temporal perception.
elements.sent-2411-070: BEV-based perception has emerged as a powerful method for enhancing object detection and tracking in autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-071: LSS~\cite{philion2020lift pioneered the use of depth estimation to project image features into BEV space, effectively addressing the challenge of fusing multi-view image data.}
elements.sent-2411-072: BEVDet4d~\cite{huang2022bevdet4d further optimized this process, while BEVDepth~\cite{li2023bevdepth and BEVStereo~\cite{li2023bevstereo improved the precision of image-to-BEV feature projection.}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Methods like Simple-BEV~\cite{harley2023simple , Fast-BEV~\cite{li2024fast , and MatrixVT~\cite{zhou2023matrixvt made BEV feature generation more efficient, while BEVFormer~\cite{li2022bevformer introduced spatial cross-attention to fuse features from different camera views in BEV space.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: These advancements laid the foundation for more sophisticated end-to-end autonomous driving systems~\cite{li2023uniformer,hu2023planning .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Additionally, recent works such as Is-Fusion~\cite{yin2024fusion , BEVFusion~\cite{liu2023bevfusion , and FusionFormer~\cite{hu2023fusionformer have focused on multi-modal data integration, combining image and LiDAR data for more accurate perception.}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: BEV-based perception offers significant advantages for autonomous driving by enabling accurate, unified spatial representations from multi-view and multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-077: We adopt BEV-based perception as it allows our system to integrate multi-sensor inputs seamlessly and accurately, supporting more reliable and robust performance in dynamic driving environments.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{images/let-vic.png} \caption{Architecture of LET-VIC. The diagram illustrates the steps involved in the LET-VIC framework. (a) and (b) Both the infrastructure-side and vehicle-side employ PointPillars to extract LiDAR point cloud features. Then the infrastructure-side features are transmitted to the vehicle side through V2X communication and integrated into the VIC cross-attention module along with the vehicle-side features. (c) The BEV encoder of LET-VIC is inspired by BEVFormer. (d) The decoder layers of LET-VIC employ TrackFormer. (e) The VIC Cross-Attention module fuses the Bird's Eye View (BEV) features from both the infrastructure and vehicle sides, compensating for calibration errors.} \label{fig:LET-VIC-framework}
elements.sent-2411-078: This section describes the LET-VIC method utilized in our proposed LiDAR-based end-to-end tracking framework for VIC.
elements.sent-2411-079: At the core of LET-VIC is the VIC Cross-Attention mechanism, which dynamically integrates LiDAR features from both vehicle-side and infrastructure-side perspectives, with a focus on each point within the ego vehicle's surrounding area.
elements.sent-2411-080: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:LET-VIC-framework , LET-VIC leverages PointPillars~\cite{lang2019pointpillars for feature extraction from both vehicle and infrastructure LiDAR data.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: The features are then fused using the VIC Cross-Attention module to enhance feature integration on the vehicle side, followed by detection and tracking using the MOTR approach~\cite{zeng2022motr .}
elements.sent-2411-082: Additionally, the framework addresses the significant impact of sensor calibration errors on detection and tracking accuracy through an online sensor calibration compensation module.
elements.sent-2411-083: We begin by defining the VIC temporal perception problem and providing relevant background information.
elements.sent-2411-084: Following this, we present our approach to generating BEV features and detail the methodology for effectively fusing multi-view data.
elements.sent-2411-085: We then address the significant challenge of sensor calibration errors and propose a compensation mechanism to mitigate their impact.
elements.sent-2411-086: Subsequently, we present our end-to-end temporal perception strategy.
elements.sent-2411-087: Finally, we discuss the evaluation metrics employed to assess the overall effectiveness of LET-VIC.
elements.sent-2411-088: VIC Temporal Perception encompasses the dual objectives of detecting and tracking objects using sequential point cloud data collected from both vehicle and infrastructure sensors.
elements.sent-2411-089: This collaborative approach enhances the overall perception of the environment, allowing for more accurate identification and monitoring of objects within the vicinity of the ego vehicle~\cite{yu2023v2x .}
elements.sent-2411-090: The study focuses specifically on point cloud data, which contains critical depth information obtained from LiDAR sensors, facilitating a robust understanding of the spatial and temporal characteristics of the objects.
elements.sent-2411-091: In the context of VIC Detection, the system is designed to identify various objects within the environment.
elements.sent-2411-092: The detection process relies on sequential point cloud data captured over time, allowing for the recognition of objects based on their spatial features.
elements.sent-2411-093: This includes determining the type of objects present, their spatial locations, dimensions and orientations.
elements.sent-2411-094: The integration of data from both vehicle and infrastructure sensors is crucial, as it provides a comprehensive view of the surroundings, improving detection accuracy and robustness against occlusions or limitations inherent to individual sensors.
elements.sent-2411-095: For the tracking aspect, VIC Tracking focuses on the continuous monitoring of detected objects as they move through the environment.
elements.sent-2411-096: This involves maintaining a unique tracking ID for each object, which allows the system to consistently identify and follow these objects over time.
elements.sent-2411-097: The tracking process is particularly challenging due to factors such as sensor noise, changes in object appearance, and the dynamic nature of the environment.
elements.sent-2411-098: By leveraging sequential data from both the vehicle and infrastructure sides, the system can achieve more stable and reliable tracking of moving objects, even in complex scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-099: The input of LET-VIC consists of two components:.
elements.sent-2411-100: Vehicle sequential point clouds ${PC_v(t_v^{' )|t_v^{'}\leq t_v}$ and their calibration matrix ${M_v(t_v^{' )|t_v^{'}\leq t_v}$, captured at and prior to time $t_v$.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Here, $PC_v(\cdot)$ denotes the vehicle-side sensor capturing function.
elements.sent-2411-102: Infrastructure sequential point clouds ${PC_i(t_i^{' )|t_i^{'}\leq t_i}$ and their calibration matrix ${M_i(t_i^{' )|t_i^{'}\leq t_i}$, captured before or at time $t_i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Here, $PC_i(\cdot)$ denotes the infrastructure-side sensor capturing function.
elements.sent-2411-104: Note that $t_i$ is earlier than $t_v$ ($t_i < t_v$) due to communication delays.
elements.sent-2411-105: The outputs of VIC Temporal Perception include the category, location, dimension, orientation, and unique tracking ID of each object in the region of interest surrounding the ego vehicle at time $t_v$.
elements.sent-2411-106: The corresponding ground truth consists of the set of tracked objects detected and tracked by any of the cooperative-view sensors at time $t_v$, which can be formulated as: \begin{equation}\setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} GT = (GT_{v} \cup GT_{i}) \cap ROI_{veh},\end{equation} where $GT_{v}$ is the ground truth from the vehicle side sensors, $GT_{i}$ is the ground truth from the infrastructure side sensors, and $ROI_{veh}$ is the ego-vehicle region of interest.
elements.sent-2411-107: Fig.~\ref{fig:pc backbone illustrates how the PointPillars framework~\cite{lang2019pointpillars is employed on both vehicle and infrastructure sides to process LiDAR point clouds.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: The raw point cloud data is first organized into pillars—grids that aggregate spatial information—enabling efficient feature extraction for subsequent processing.
elements.sent-2411-109: The pipeline for BEV feature generation begins by preprocessing the 3D point clouds (x, y, z).
elements.sent-2411-110: In the voxelization step, the point clouds are discretized into 3D grid cells (voxels), with distinct regions of interest (ROI) for vehicle and infrastructure sides.
elements.sent-2411-111: The vehicle side ROI spans x: [-51.2, 51.2] meters, y: [-51.2, 51.2] meters, and z: [-5.0, 3.0] meters, while the infrastructure side ROI covers x: [0, 102.4] meters, y: [-51.2, 51.2] meters, and z: [-5.0, 3.0] meters.
elements.sent-2411-112: Voxelization uses a uniform voxel size of 0.2 meters along the x and y axes, and 8 meters along the z-axis.
elements.sent-2411-113: As a result, the vehicle-side grid is $512\times512$, and the infrastructure-side grid is also $512\times512$.
elements.sent-2411-114: Each voxel stores its corresponding points and their coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-115: Following voxelization, PillarFeatureNet extracts high-dimensional features from the voxels, transforming the point cloud into a 2D BEV representation.
elements.sent-2411-116: These pseudo-image features are scattered using the PointPillarsScatter layer and are further refined by SECOND convolutional layers.
elements.sent-2411-117: Finally, a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) integrates multi-scale features, outputting a set of multi-level features to enhance object perception capabilities across different spatial resolutions.
elements.sent-2411-118: In V2X communication, three primary data types are transmitted: raw data, instance-level perception data, and feature-level intermediate data.
elements.sent-2411-119: Each type incurs different transmission costs and offers varying levels of information retention.
elements.sent-2411-120: Raw data includes unprocessed sensor information, like LiDAR point clouds or images.
elements.sent-2411-121: It has the highest transmission cost due to its large volume but retains the most information as it is unfiltered and uncompressed.
elements.sent-2411-122: Instance-level perception data consists of processed outputs like detected objects and attributes or queries that contain perception results but are not yet decoded~\cite{fan2023quest .}
elements.sent-2411-123: It has the lowest transmission cost due to high compression, but this also leads to the highest information loss because it transmits only selected information.
elements.sent-2411-124: Feature-level intermediate data comprises key features extracted from raw data, such as BEV features~\cite{chen2023transiff .}
elements.sent-2411-125: It balances transmission costs and information retention.
elements.sent-2411-126: Our V2X communication framework prioritizes transmitting BEV features due to the trade-off between transmission cost and information retention.
elements.sent-2411-127: BEV features capture essential spatial relationships and environmental context with reduced data volume, ensuring critical information for accurate perception and decision-making is retained.
elements.sent-2411-128: This approach significantly reduces the required communication bandwidth compared to raw data transmission.
elements.sent-2411-129: Additionally, the VIC Cross-Attention mechanism effectively integrates BEV features from both vehicle and infrastructure sides.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.24\textwidth]{images/let-vic-pc-backbone.png} \caption{PointCloud Backbone. The PointCloud Backbone extract Bird's Eye View (BEV) features from point cloud data. The pipeline starts with data preprocessing, voxelization, and feature extraction via PillarFeatureNet. Pseudo-image features are then processed by SECOND and FPN, producing multi-level features for object perception at various scales.} \label{fig:pc backbone}
elements.sent-2411-130: The deformable attention mechanism plays a critical role in LET-VIC's ability to dynamically fuse features from multiple viewpoints.
elements.sent-2411-131: Traditional attention mechanisms can struggle with multi-view fusion due to high computational costs and difficulties in aligning spatial features across different perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-132: In contrast, deformable attention significantly reduces computational overhead by focusing on a sparse set of key features, known as reference points, and dynamically adjusting its focus based on local feature distributions.
elements.sent-2411-133: In LET-VIC, the VIC Cross-Attention Module employs deformable attention to integrate BEV features from both vehicle-side and infrastructure-side sensors.
elements.sent-2411-134: The process begins by projecting BEV queries to each perspective and generating initial reference points.
elements.sent-2411-135: A learnable network then predicts calibration adjustments to refine these reference points, compensating for any misalignments between the two perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-136: Deformable attention is subsequently applied around the refined points, enabling the model to sample key features efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-137: This approach ensures that only the most relevant features from both viewpoints are fused, enhancing the efficiency and accuracy of feature integration while accounting for sensor misalignments.
elements.sent-2411-138: The ability to adaptively focus on critical features enables the deformable attention mechanism to excel in complex, multi-view tracking tasks where spatial alignment may vary due to sensor calibration errors.
elements.sent-2411-139: Fig.~\ref{fig:vic cross-attention illustrates the VIC Cross-Attention mechanism, showcasing its ability to dynamically integrate data across different sensors.}
elements.sent-2411-140: The process of VIC Cross-Attention (VICCrossAttn) can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:vic cross attention} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} VICCrossAttn(Q^{bev},F^{veh},F^{inf},P^{veh}, P^{inf})=\notag \sum_{i=1}^{N_{ref}}\left[\frac{1}{1+M_{i}^{inf}}\left(MSDeformAttn(Q_i^{bev},P_i^{veh},F^{veh})\right.\right.\notag +\left.\left.M_{i}^{inf}\cdotMSDeformAttn(Q_i^{bev},P_i^{inf},F^{inf})\right)\right],\end{equation} where $i$ indexes the reference points, and $N_{ref}$ is the total reference points for BEV Query $Q^{bev}$.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{images/vic-cross-attention.png} \caption{VIC Cross-Attention. The VIC Cross-Attention module fuses the Bird's Eye View (BEV) features from both the infrastructure and vehicle sides, compensating for calibration errors. First, We project BEV queries to both the infrastructure side and vehicle side features to get the original reference point. Then, we use a learnable network to predict the calibration compensation to refine the original reference point. Finally, we use deformable attention sampling around the corrected reference point to generate the respective BEV feature.} \label{fig:vic cross-attention}
elements.sent-2411-141: The multi-scale deformable attention (MSDeformAttn) function is defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:msdeformattn} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} MSDeformAttn(Q_i,{P}_i,\{F^l\}_{l=1}^L)\notag =\sum_{m=1}^M{W_m}\Bigg[\sum_{l=1}^L\sum_{k=1}^K A_{mlqk}\cdot{W'_m}F_i^l(R_{l}({P}_i)+\Delta{p_{mlqk}})\Bigg],\end{equation} Where $i\in\Omega_i$ indexes a query element with representation feature $Q_i\in\mathbb{R ^C$, where $C$ is the feature dimension.}
elements.sent-2411-142: The scalar attention weight $A_{mlqk}$ is normalized by $\sum_{l=1 ^L\sum_{k=1 ^K{A_{mlqk =1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: The function $\mathcal{R _l({P _i)$ re-scales the normalized coordinates ${P _i$ to the input feature map of the $l$-th level.}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: Calibration errors in sensors, arising from precision variations, environmental factors, and calibration method uncertainties, pose significant challenges to perception accuracy in autonomous driving systems.
elements.sent-2411-145: Compensating for sensor calibration errors is crucial for LET-VIC as it ensures accurate data fusion and reliable cooperative perception performance.
elements.sent-2411-146: Therefore, we implemented CEC module to optimize spatial error correction during coordinate transformation, delivering precise and robust temporal perception.
elements.sent-2411-147: Initially, the positions of vehicle-side or infrastructure-side features corresponding to each point in the BEV features are determined through coordinate system transformation.
elements.sent-2411-148: Subsequently, CEC employs a set of learnable parameters that adapt during training to learn adaptive offsets, which include ${\Delta x ^{inf}$, ${\Delta y ^{inf}$, ${\Delta x ^{veh}$, and ${\Delta y ^{veh}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Finally, the positions of vehicle-side or roadside features corresponding to each BEV feature are adjusted by adding this set of offsets, forming compensated reference points as shown in Eqs.~\ref{eq:veh error offsets and ~\ref{eq:inf error offsets .}}
elements.sent-2411-150: Then, we normalize the original reference point coordinates according to the specified point cloud range, as detailed in Eqs.~\ref{eq:normalized veh error offsets and ~\ref{eq:normalized inf error offsets . \begin{equation}\label{eq:veh error offsets} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} x_i^{veh} y_i^{veh}=R_{bev2veh}\cdot{Ref}_i^{bev}+T_{bev2veh}+ {\Delta x}_i^{veh} {\Delta y}_i^{veh} ,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:inf error offsets} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} x_i^{inf} y_i^{inf}=R_{bev2inf}\cdot{Ref}_i^{bev}+T_{bev2inf}+ {\Delta x}_i^{inf} {\Delta y}_i^{inf} ,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:normalized veh error offsets} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} {P}_i^{veh}= \cfrac{x_i^{veh}-x_{min}^{veh}}{x_{max}^{veh}-x_{min}^{veh}} \cfrac{y_i^{veh}-y_{min}^{veh}}{y_{max}^{veh}-y_{min}^{veh}} ,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:normalized inf error offsets} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} {P}_i^{inf}= \cfrac{x_i^{inf}-x_{min}^{inf}}{x_{max}^{inf}-x_{min}^{inf}} \cfrac{y_i^{inf}-y_{min}^{inf}}{y_{max}^{inf}-y_{min}^{inf}} ,\end{equation} Where $x_i^{veh}$, $y_i^{veh}$ represents the $i$-th original coordinates of the reference point on the vehicle-side and $x_i^{inf}$, $y_i^{inf}$ represents the $i$-th original coordinates of the reference point on the infrastructure-side.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: The end-to-end tracking capability of LET-VIC is powered by an advanced architecture inspired by the TrackFormer framework~\cite{zeng2022motr,meinhardt2022trackformer,hu2023planning , which unifies object detection and tracking tasks within a single Transformer-based pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-152: This integration bypasses the conventional two-step tracking-by-detection approach, where detection is followed by post-processing for tracking.
elements.sent-2411-153: Instead, LET-VIC directly associates objects across frames during detection, allowing for joint optimization of both tasks.
elements.sent-2411-154: Within LET-VIC, detect queries and track queries play distinct but complementary roles in the TrackFormer decoder.
elements.sent-2411-155: Detect queries are responsible for identifying new objects in each frame by interacting with the current frame's features, focusing on objects not previously tracked.
elements.sent-2411-156: Track queries, on the other hand, are used to maintain object identities across frames, carrying information from previous frames to ensure temporal consistency by associating the same object across consecutive frames.
elements.sent-2411-157: This combination allows for simultaneous detection and tracking, ensuring seamless object tracking throughout sequences.
elements.sent-2411-158: LET-VIC's end-to-end design benefits from a joint loss function that simultaneously optimizes detection and tracking.
elements.sent-2411-159: The loss function includes components for object classification, bounding box regression, and identity preservation.
elements.sent-2411-160: This comprehensive approach enhances overall accuracy and ensures that objects are correctly identified and tracked even in complex and dynamic environments.
elements.sent-2411-161: To evaluate LET-VIC's performance, we selected a range of metrics that comprehensively assess both detection and tracking accuracy~\cite{caesar2020nuscenes,geiger2013vision,weng20203d .}
elements.sent-2411-162: These metrics are standard in 3D tracking benchmarks and provide a balanced view of system performance under various conditions:.
elements.sent-2411-163: It measures how accurately detected objects match ground-truth objects across different recall levels, typically using distance thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-164: For each distance threshold, precision-recall curves are generated, and the area under each curve gives the Average Precision (AP).
elements.sent-2411-165: The final mAP score is the mean of AP values across all classes and distance thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-166: A higher mAP indicates better detection quality.
elements.sent-2411-167: The formulations for AP and mAP are: \begin{equation}\label{eq:map} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} AP=\int_{0}^{1}precision(r)dr,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:map} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} mAP=\cfrac{1}{N}\sum_{c=1}^{N}AP_c,\end{equation} Where $r$ represents recall levels from 0 to 1, $N$ is the number of classes, and $\text{AP _c$ is the average precision for class $c$.}
elements.sent-2411-168: In practice, the area under the PR curve is often approximated by taking the precision at discrete recall levels.
elements.sent-2411-169: AMOTA (Average Multi-Object Tracking Accuracy): AMOTA is a central metric for evaluating tracking accuracy in multi-object scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-170: It builds on Multi-Object Tracking Accuracy (MOTA), which assesses tracking accuracy by accounting for false positives, false negatives, and identity switches between tracked objects and ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-171: AMOTA averages MOTA scores across different difficulty levels and recall thresholds, providing an overall measure of tracking accuraciy.
elements.sent-2411-172: Higher AMOTA values are preferred, as they reflect consistent tracking accuracy across recall levels.
elements.sent-2411-173: The formulations for MOTA and AMOTA are: \begin{equation}\label{eq:mota} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} MOTA=1-\cfrac{\sum_{t}(FP_t+FN_t+IDS_t)}{\sum_{t}(GT_t)},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:amota} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} AMOTA=\cfrac{1}{R}\sum_{r=1}^{R}MOTA_r,\end{equation} Where $\text{FP _t$ is the number of false positives at time $t$, $\text{FN _t$ is the number of false negatives at time $t$, $\text{IDS _t$ is the number of ID switches at time $t$, $\text{GT _t$ is the total number of groundtruth objects at time $t$, $R$ is the number of recall levels, and $\text{MOTA _r$ is the MOTA score at recall level $r$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-174: AMOTP (Average Multi-Object Tracking Precision): AMOTP is an essential metric for evaluating tracking precision in multi-object scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-175: It builds on Multi-Object Tracking Precision (MOTP), which measures the spatial alignment between tracked and ground-truth object positions by calculating the average positional error.
elements.sent-2411-176: AMOTP averages MOTP scores across different difficulty levels and recall thresholds, providing an overall measure of tracking precision.
elements.sent-2411-177: Lower AMOTP values are preferred, as they reflect consistent tracking precision across recall levels.
elements.sent-2411-178: The formulations for MOTP and AMOTP are: \begin{equation}\label{eq:motp} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} MOTP=\cfrac{\sum_{i,t}d_{i,t}}{\sum_{t}(c_t)},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:amotp} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} AMOTP=\cfrac{1}{R}\sum_{r=1}^{R}MOTP_r,\end{equation} Where $d_{i,t}$ is the positional error for the $i$-th matched object at time $t$, $c_t$ is the number of successfully matched object pairs at time $t$, $R$ is the number of recall levels, and $\text{MOTP _r$ is the MOTP score at recall level $r$.}
elements.sent-2411-179: These metrics offer a thorough evaluation of LET-VIC's detection and tracking performance under varied conditions.
elements.sent-2411-180: We select mAP as our primary detection metric because it effectively measures the accuracy of object identification, providing insight into the model's ability to detect objects with precision.
elements.sent-2411-181: For tracking, we emphasize AMOTA and AMOTP as they capture tracking accuracy and spatial precision across multiple recall thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-182: These metrics provide a comprehensive assessment of LET-VIC's robustness and consistency, especially in complex, real-world environments where accurate object identification and stable tracking are critical.

elements.sent-2411-205: The comparative results of LET-VIC and LET-V demonstrate that LET-VIC consistently outperforms LET-V under various delay conditions, highlighting its strong robustness to latency.
elements.sent-2411-206: Leveraging the VIC Cross-Attention module, LET-VIC effectively integrates BEV features from infrastructure sensors, significantly enhancing detection accuracy and tracking performance with minimal degradation, even under delayed conditions.
elements.sent-2411-207: Specifically, in detection performance, LET-VIC achieves substantial improvements over LET-V, with +15.0\% mAP at 0ms, +12.3\% mAP at 100ms, +9.8\% mAP at 200ms and +7.0\% mAP at 300ms.
elements.sent-2411-208: For tracking performance, LET-VIC outperforms LET-V with +17.3\% AMOTA at 0 ms, +16.4\% AMOTA at 100 ms, +14.6\% AMOTA at 200 ms and +10.3\% AMOTA at 300 Ms. Moreover, LET-VIC consistently achieves lower AMOTP values than LET-V, with -0.413 AMOTP at 0 ms, -0.397 AMOTP at 100 ms, -0.338 AMOTP at 200 ms and -0.302 AMOTP at 300 ms, reflecting superior localization accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-209: Additionally, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig: perception_performance_fig , although each metric shows a slight decline within a latency range up to 300 ms, LET-VIC still surpasses LET-V by +7.0\% in mAP, +10.3\% in AMOTA, and -0.302 in AMOTP at 300 ms delay, demonstrating its ability to effectively fuse infrastructure features under certain delays and enhance perception capabilities, showcasing robustness to latency.}

elements.sent-2411-216: This section investigates the impact of our CEC module on perception accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-217: Calibration errors can significantly degrade tracking performance, and our module is designed to mitigate these effects.
elements.sent-2411-218: We compare the performance of the LET-VIC model with and without the CEC module under delays of 0 ms, 100 ms, 200 ms, and 300 Ms. The dataset annotations are used without manual error compensation to highlight the module's effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-219: Due to limited computational resources, this section of experiments extracts the V2X-Seq-SPD dataset at a reduced frequency of 2Hz to generate the V2X-Seq-SPD-2Hz dataset.
elements.sent-2411-220: Additionally, we reduced the feature dimensionality in the model from 256 dimensions to 64 dimensions to expedite training and evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-221: Other settings remain consistent with those described in Section~\ref{sec:let-vic implementation details .}
elements.sent-2411-222: The results presented in Table~\ref{tab:SC Tracking Benchmark results demonstrate that LET-VIC with the CEC module consistently outperforms the standard LET-VIC across all delay settings, highlighting the CEC module's effectiveness in enhancing perception robustness.}
elements.sent-2411-223: Specifically, LET-VIC with calibration error compensation achieves +6.4\% mAP and +4.7\% AMOTA improvements, along with a -0.038 AMOTP reduction at 0 ms delay; +7.5\% mAP, +5.5\% AMOTA, and -0.079 AMOTP at 100 ms delay; +5.0\% mAP, +1.8\% AMOTA, and -0.044 AMOTP at 200 ms delay; and +5.2\% mAP, +4.2\% AMOTA, and -0.042 AMOTP at 300 ms delay.
elements.sent-2411-224: By compensating for sensor calibration errors, this module improves multi-view feature fusion, leading to better overall performance and demonstrating LET-VIC's robustness to calibration inaccuracies.
elements.sent-2411-225: This paper introduces LET-VIC, a LiDAR-based end-to-end tracking framework for VIC.
elements.sent-2411-226: By integrating information from multi-view sensor sources, LET-VIC addresses the limitations of single-view perception and enhances the system's ability to detect and track objects.
elements.sent-2411-227: Through its end-to-end design, LET-VIC effectively incorporates temporal information to maintain object identity across frames in both detection and tracking, significantly improving performance in dynamic environments.
elements.sent-2411-228: Moreover, its feature fusion approach compensates for spatial calibration errors during vehicle-infrastructure coordinate transformations, ensuring robust and accurate object perception.
elements.sent-2411-229: Experimental results on the V2X-Seq-SPD dataset confirm the effectiveness of this approach, demonstrating substantial improvements in tracking performance and system robustness.
elements.sent-2411-230: LET-VIC, while effective, is limited by relying solely on point cloud inputs from LiDAR sensors.
elements.sent-2411-231: Although point clouds provide accurate depth and 3D structure, they lack color and texture details, affecting the recognition of fine object attributes.
elements.sent-2411-232: Additionally, point clouds can become sparse at longer distances or in complex environments, impacting detection and tracking accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-233: Conversely, image data offers rich semantic information but lacks depth.
elements.sent-2411-234: Future work will focus on fusing point cloud and image features to enhance both spatial perception and semantic understanding, improving overall system performance in complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-235: Another limitation is the lack of compensation for communication delays in VIC, which can affect real-time tracking performance.
elements.sent-2411-236: Future research will address this by developing strategies to mitigate temporal delays, ensuring more accurate and robust tracking across multi-view sensors.. { \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}}
elements.sent-2411-237: Temporal perception, defined as the capability to detect and track objects across temporal sequences, serves as a fundamental component in autonomous driving systems.
elements.sent-2411-238: While single-vehicle perception systems encounter limitations, stemming from incomplete perception due to object occlusion and inherent blind spots, cooperative perception systems present their own challenges in terms of sensor calibration precision and positioning accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-239: First, we employ Temporal Self-Attention and VIC Cross-Attention modules to effectively integrate temporal and spatial information from both vehicle and infrastructure perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-240: Then, we develop a novel Calibration Error Compensation (CEC) module to mitigate sensor misalignment issues and facilitate accurate feature alignment.
elements.sent-2411-241: Experiments on the V2X-Seq-SPD dataset demonstrate that LET-VIC significantly outperforms baseline models.
elements.sent-2411-242: Compared to LET-V, LET-VIC achieves +15.0\% improvement in mAP and a +17.3\% improvement in AMOTA.
elements.sent-2411-243: Furthermore, LET-VIC surpasses representative Tracking by Detection models, including V2VNet, FFNet, and PointPillars, with at least a +13.7\% improvement in mAP and a +13.1\% improvement in AMOTA without considering communication delays, showcasing its robust detection and tracking performance.
elements.sent-2411-244: The experiments demonstrate that the integration of multi-view perspectives, temporal sequences, or CEC in end-to-end training significantly improves both detection and tracking performance.
elements.sent-2411-246: The core innovation lies in simultaneously enhancing perception through two complementary dimensions: spatially fusing infrastructure perspectives with vehicle sensors to expand coverage, and temporally integrating historical observations to resolve occlusion ambiguity, both of which are unified within one efficient framework.
elements.sent-2411-247: This dual-fusion approach, which combines cross-sensor spatial fusion with temporal evolution modeling, enables the system to overcome both physical obstructions and single-frame sensing limitations.
elements.sent-2411-248: Compared to existing approaches, LET-VIC is novel in its ability to unify spatial and temporal data fusion in an end-to-end manner.
elements.sent-2411-249: This ensures not only better detection and tracking performance but also improved robustness to environmental complexity and sensor misalignments, making it a critical step forward for autonomous driving applications.
elements.sent-2411-250: Cooperative perception in autonomous driving enhances perception capabilities to address challenges such as occlusion, long-distance perception, and sensor failures that single-vehicle perception may struggle with.
elements.sent-2411-251: Recent cooperative perception research primarily focuses on two types: Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) and Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) cooperation~\cite{sehla2022resource,cheng2022integrated,jang2023study,lu2024integrated .}
elements.sent-2411-252: In V2V cooperation, different fusion approaches have been explored.
elements.sent-2411-253: On the other hand, V2I cooperation has been explored through models such as V2X-ViT~\cite{xu2022v2x , FFNet~\cite{yu2023flow , CTCE~\cite{zhong2024leveraging , V2X-Graph~\cite{ruan2023learning and UniV2X{yu2024end , which adopt a feature fusion approach to integrate data from both vehicles and infrastructure to provide broader environmental awareness.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-254: The paradigm of End-to-End Temporal Perception has redefined sequential object perception by shifting from traditional tracking-by-detection to a unified model which has been proven to simultaneously enhance both detection and tracking accuracy~\cite{zeng2022motr,zhang2022mutr3d,zhang2023motrv2,yu2023motrv3,lin2023sparse4dv3,li2023end .}
elements.sent-2411-255: However, the end-to-end framework has not been thoroughly explored in the LiDAR-based VIC field.
elements.sent-2411-256: HYDRO-3D~\cite{meng2023hydro improve detection accuracy by leveraging temporal information and collaborative perception, yet they lack a comprehensive evaluation of tracking performance.}
elements.sent-2411-257: MOT-CUP~\cite{su2024collaborative proposes a joint perception post-processing framework validated under ideal simulation V2X-Sim dataset~\cite{li2022v2x .}}
elements.sent-2411-258: However, the effectiveness of real-time collaborative perception and tracking is significantly impacted by transmission delays, data timestamp alignment, and the precision of coordinate transformation matrices, all of which are critical and non-negligible factors.
elements.sent-2411-259: These issues substantially increase the complexity of hyperparameter tuning for such post-processing algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-260: In contrast, end-to-end training addresses these challenges by employing sophisticated network designs, eliminating the need for manual hyperparameter configuration while achieving greater robustness.
elements.sent-2411-261: However, these approaches solely focus on optimizing the tracking component and fail to improve detection accuracy as same as MOT-CUP~\cite{su2024collaborative .}
elements.sent-2411-262: To bridge these gaps, we introduce a novel LiDAR-based end-to-end temporal perception framework for VIC, which leverages multi-view fusion between vehicle-side and infrastructure-side sensors.
elements.sent-2411-263: By integrating complementary spatial and temporal information, this framework significantly enhances perception accuracy and robustness, marking a significant advance in cooperative temporal perception.
elements.sent-2411-264: BEV-based perception has emerged as a powerful method for enhancing object detection and tracking in autonomous driving by enabling accurate, unified spatial representations from multi-view and multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-265: Unlike the BEVDet series of methods, BEVFormer~\cite{li2022bevformer introduced spatial cross-attention to fuse features from different camera views in BEV space.}
elements.sent-2411-266: Methods like Simple-BEV~\cite{harley2023simple , Fast-BEV~\cite{li2024fast , and MatrixVT~\cite{zhou2023matrixvt have made BEV feature generation more efficient.}}}
elements.sent-2411-267: These advancements laid the foundation for more sophisticated end-to-end autonomous driving systems~\cite{li2023uniformer,hu2023planning,zhang2024graphad,ye2023fusionad,sun2024sparsedrive,yu2024end .}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{images/let-vic.png} \caption{Architecture of LET-VIC. The diagram illustrates the steps involved in the LET-VIC framework. (a) and (b) Both the infrastructure-side and vehicle-side employ PointPillars~\cite{lang2019pointpillars} to extract LiDAR point cloud features. Then the infrastructure-side features are transmitted to the vehicle side through V2X communication and integrated into the VIC cross-attention module along with the vehicle-side features. (c) The BEV encoder of LET-VIC is inspired by BEVFormer. (d) The decoder layers of LET-VIC employ TrackFormer. (e) The VIC Cross-Attention module fuses the Bird's Eye View (BEV) features from both the infrastructure and vehicle sides, compensating for calibration errors.} \label{fig:LET-VIC-framework}
elements.sent-2411-268: LET-VIC's key novelty lies in dynamically combining LiDAR data from both vehicle and infrastructure perspectives through a novel VIC Cross-Attention mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-269: This design enables more accurate object detection and tracking by addressing challenges such as sensor calibration errors and limited individual sensor coverage.
elements.sent-2411-270: In essence, LET-VIC enhances environmental awareness, enabling safer and more efficient autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-271: The end-to-end design further simplifies the traditional tracking pipeline, combining object detection and tracking into a seamless, jointly optimized framework, improving efficiency and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-272: Figure~\ref{fig:pc backbone provides a detailed illustration of the Pointcloud Backbone used in both the vehicle-side and infrastructure-side components of the LET-VIC framework, corresponding to parts (a) and (b) in Fig.~\ref{fig:LET-VIC-framework .}}
elements.sent-2411-273: The diagram shows how the PointPillars framework~\cite{lang2019pointpillars is employed to process LiDAR point clouds on both sides.}
elements.sent-2411-274: The block diagram between parts (a) and (b) of Fig.~\ref{fig:LET-VIC-framework illustrates the transmission of BEV features from the infrastructure side to the vehicle side.}
elements.sent-2411-275: Figure~\ref{fig:vic cross-attention corresponds to part (e) in Fig.~\ref{fig:LET-VIC-framework and illustrates the VIC Cross-Attention mechanism, showcasing its ability to dynamically integrate data across different sensors.}}
elements.sent-2411-276: The CEC module is integrated into the VIC Cross-Attention mechanism, which corresponds to part (e) in Fig.~\ref{fig:LET-VIC-framework .}
elements.sent-2411-277: Finally, the positions of vehicle-side or roadside features corresponding to each BEV feature are adjusted by adding this set of offsets, forming compensated reference points as shown in (\ref{eq:veh error offsets ) and (\ref{eq:inf error offsets ).}}
elements.sent-2411-278: Then, we normalize the original reference point coordinates according to the specified point cloud range, as detailed in (\ref{eq:normalized veh error offsets ) and (\ref{eq:normalized inf error offsets ). \begin{equation}\label{eq:veh error offsets} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} x_i^{veh} y_i^{veh}=R_{bev2veh}\cdot{Ref}_i^{bev}+T_{bev2veh}+ {\Delta x}_i^{veh} {\Delta y}_i^{veh} ,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:inf error offsets} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} x_i^{inf} y_i^{inf}=R_{bev2inf}\cdot{Ref}_i^{bev}+T_{bev2inf}+ {\Delta x}_i^{inf} {\Delta y}_i^{inf} ,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:normalized veh error offsets} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} {P}_i^{veh}= \cfrac{x_i^{veh}-x_{min}^{veh}}{x_{max}^{veh}-x_{min}^{veh}} \cfrac{y_i^{veh}-y_{min}^{veh}}{y_{max}^{veh}-y_{min}^{veh}} ,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq:normalized inf error offsets} \setlength\abovedisplayskip{0.05cm} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{0.15cm} {P}_i^{inf}= \cfrac{x_i^{inf}-x_{min}^{inf}}{x_{max}^{inf}-x_{min}^{inf}} \cfrac{y_i^{inf}-y_{min}^{inf}}{y_{max}^{inf}-y_{min}^{inf}} ,\end{equation} Where $x_i^{veh}$, $y_i^{veh}$ represents the $i$-th original coordinates of the reference point on the vehicle-side and $x_i^{inf}$, $y_i^{inf}$ represents the $i$-th original coordinates of the reference point on the infrastructure-side.}}
elements.sent-2411-279: The end-to-end tracking capability of LET-VIC, shown in part (d) of Fig.~\ref{fig:LET-VIC-framework , is powered by an advanced architecture inspired by the TrackFormer framework~\cite{zeng2022motr,meinhardt2022trackformer,hu2023planning , which unifies object detection and tracking tasks within a single Transformer-based pipeline.}}
elements.sent-2411-280: We conduct experiments on two datasets: the real-world V2X-Seq-SPD dataset~\cite{yu2023v2x and the simulated V2X-Sim dataset~\cite{li2022v2x , collected from Carla.}}
elements.sent-2411-281: V2X-Seq-SPD~\cite{yu2023v2x is a real-world dataset designed for VIC temporal perception.}
elements.sent-2411-282: It consists of 15,371 pairs of cooperative frames captured at 10Hz across 95 scenes.
elements.sent-2411-283: Each frame pair contains synchronized sensor data from both vehicle-side and infrastructure-side perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-284: We conduct training on 7,445 frame pairs from 46 scenes and evaluate on 3,316 frame pairs from 21 validation scenes.
elements.sent-2411-285: This dataset is used for comparative experiments and ablation studies, with results reported on the validation set.
elements.sent-2411-286: V2X-Sim~\cite{li2022v2x is a simulation dataset collected from virtual environments in Carla.}
elements.sent-2411-287: It comprises 10,000 cooperative frames recorded at 5Hz across 100 scenes.
elements.sent-2411-288: To validate the generalizability of our approach, we conduct VIC temporal perception experiments on this dataset.
elements.sent-2411-289: In each scene, we select one road-side unit (RSU) and one CAV to establish cooperative perception conditions.
elements.sent-2411-290: We train on 5,000 frame pairs from 50 scenes and evaluate on 2,000 frame pairs from 20 validation scenes.
elements.sent-2411-291: We extract V2X-Seq-SPD~\cite{yu2023v2x at a frequency of 10Hz to create the V2X-Seq-SPD-10Hz dataset~\cite{yu2023v2x , aligning with nuScenes~\cite{caesar2020nuscenes .}}}
elements.sent-2411-292: We also evaluate our method on the V2X-Sim dataset~\cite{li2022v2x , which is extracted at 5Hz.}
elements.sent-2411-293: To establish cooperative perception conditions, we select one RSU and one CAV per scene.
elements.sent-2411-294: The ego vehicle-side BEV range configuration remains the same as in V2X-Seq-SPD~\cite{yu2023v2x , while the infrastructure-side BEV range configuration is set to \([-51.2, 51.2, -51.2, 51.2]\) meters, with the same grid resolution of 0.512m $\times$ 0.512m.}
elements.sent-2411-295: To assess the computational feasibility of LET-VIC, we analyze its complexity in terms of floating point operations per second (FLOPs) and parameter count.
elements.sent-2411-296: LET-VIC requires 514.86 G FLOPs and consists of 30.11 M parameters.
elements.sent-2411-297: These values reflect the model's capability to efficiently leverage vehicle-infrastructure cooperation while maintaining practical computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-298: The balance between accuracy and efficiency makes LET-VIC well-suited for real-world deployment.

elements.sent-2411-308: As shown in the figure, LET-VIC demonstrates robust object tracking across frames, as evidenced by the consistent color-coding of tracked objects, while the noticeably fewer semi-transparent gray dashed missed-detection boxes further underscore its enhanced perception capability and reduced detection failures.
elements.sent-2411-309: This improvement is primarily attributed to vehicle-infrastructure coorperation, where infrastructure-side sensors supply complementary perception data, effectively mitigating blind spots and occlusions in the vehicle's field of view.
elements.sent-2411-310: Through the VIC Cross-Attention module, LET-VIC seamlessly integrates multi-view BEV features from infrastructure sensors, bridging information gaps in the single-vehicle perspective.
elements.sent-2411-311: This integration significantly enhances detection accuracy and tracking performance while maintaining robustness, even under delayed conditions.
elements.sent-2411-312: Furthermore, our experiments on the V2X-Sim dataset~\cite{li2022v2x validate the generalizability of our approach under more challenging cooperative conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-313: Unlike the V2X-Seq-SPD dataset~\cite{yu2023v2x , which offers dense collaboration with a 10Hz sampling frequency and full-frame cooperation, V2X-Sim~\cite{li2022v2x operates at a lower temporal resolution (5Hz) and exhibits sparser cooperation—only 87.2\% of frames contain valid collaborative perception due to limited sensor overlap between the RSU and CAV.}}
elements.sent-2411-314: Despite these constraints, LET-VIC still achieves notable improvements over LET-V, with an increase of +2.2\% in mAP and +6.7\% in AMOTA.
elements.sent-2411-315: This performance gain under partial cooperation underscores LET-VIC's capability to effectively leverage intermittent infrastructure signals and maintain robust perception even when continuous collaboration is not feasible.
elements.sent-2411-316: Overall, the consistent performance across these diverse datasets confirms the adaptability of our approach to different sensor configurations and collaboration frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-317: The performance improvement of LET-VIC is due to its ability to fuse temporal history information.
elements.sent-2411-318: This allows previously visible objects' features to be carried forward, compensating for occlusions in the current frame.
elements.sent-2411-319: For example, targets visible in prior frames but obscured in the current frame benefit from temporal feature integration, resulting in enhanced detection and tracking performance.
elements.sent-2411-320: This capability is particularly beneficial in addressing blind spots in the current frame by leveraging features from previous observations.
elements.sent-2411-321: For tracking performance, LET-VIC outperforms V2VNet~\cite{wang2020v2vnet , FFNet~\cite{yu2023flow , and PointPillars~\cite{lang2019pointpillars with at least +13.1\% AMOTA at 0 ms, +12.1\% AMOTA at 100 ms, +12.7\% AMOTA at 200 ms and +9.2\% AMOTA at 300 Ms. Moreover, LET-VIC consistently achieves lower AMOTP values, with at least -0.122 AMOTP at 0 ms, -0.117 AMOTP at 100 ms, -0.100 AMOTP at 200 ms and -0.063 AMOTP at 300 ms, reflecting superior localization accuracy.}}}
elements.sent-2411-322: Additionally, Compared to V2VNet~\cite{wang2020v2vnet , FFNet~\cite{yu2023flow , and PointPillars~\cite{lang2019pointpillars , LET-V demonstrates that even with single-vehicle perception, the end-to-end approach significantly improves detection and tracking.}}}
elements.sent-2411-323: Its performance approaches that of PointPillars~\cite{lang2019pointpillars and surpasses both FFNet~\cite{yu2023flow and V2VNet~\cite{wang2020v2vnet .}}}

elements.sent-2411-325: By compensating for sensor calibration errors, the CEC module improves the accuracy of multi-view feature fusion.
elements.sent-2411-326: This ensures that the LiDAR feature points from the vehicle and infrastructure sides, corresponding to the same real-world location, are correctly aligned.
elements.sent-2411-327: Without such compensation, misalignments between vehicle-side and infrastructure-side feature points may introduce noise during feature fusion, negatively impacting perception performance.
